tiles.name: Chinatown
tiles.funFact: Manhattan's Chinatown is the largest in the Western Hemisphere, home to over 90,000 residents and more than 200 restaurants.

tiles.name: Lower East Side
tiles.funFact: In the early 1900s, the Lower East Side was the most densely populated neighborhood in the world, housing waves of immigrants from Europe.

tiles.name: Grand Central
tiles.funFact: Grand Central Terminal has 44 platforms and 67 tracks, making it the largest train station in the world by number of platforms.

tiles.name: SoHo
tiles.funFact: SoHo stands for "South of Houston Street" and contains the largest collection of cast-iron architecture in the world.

tiles.name: Greenwich Village
tiles.funFact: Greenwich Village was the birthplace of the American bohemian movement and the 1969 Stonewall uprising that launched the modern LGBTQ rights movement.

tiles.name: Chelsea
tiles.funFact: The High Line, a 1.45-mile elevated park built on a former freight rail line, runs through Chelsea and attracts over 8 million visitors annually.

tiles.name: East Village
tiles.funFact: The East Village was the epicenter of the 1960s counterculture movement and punk rock scene, with legendary venue CBGB hosting the Ramones and Blondie.

tiles.name: Con Edison
tiles.funFact: Consolidated Edison traces its origins to Thomas Edison's first commercial power station on Pearl Street in 1882, which lit up lower Manhattan.

tiles.name: Times Square
tiles.funFact: Times Square's iconic billboards use enough electricity to power 161 homes for a year, and nearly 50 million people visit the area annually.

tiles.name: Midtown
tiles.funFact: Midtown Manhattan has the highest concentration of skyscrapers in the world, including the Empire State Building, Chrysler Building, and One Vanderbilt.

tiles.name: Penn Station
tiles.funFact: Penn Station is the busiest transportation hub in the Western Hemisphere, serving over 600,000 passengers daily across Amtrak, LIRR, and NJ Transit.

tiles.name: Hell's Kitchen
tiles.funFact: Hell's Kitchen got its name in the 1880s when a police officer said "this place is hell itself," and the neighborhood became known for its tough reputation.

tiles.name: Wall Street
tiles.funFact: Wall Street is named after an actual wall built by Dutch colonists in 1653 to protect New Amsterdam from British invasion. The NYSE trades over $20 trillion annually.

tiles.name: Brooklyn Bridge
tiles.funFact: When the Brooklyn Bridge opened in 1883, it was the longest suspension bridge in the world at 1,595 feet, and six days later 12 people died in a stampede caused by panic.

tiles.name: Central Park
tiles.funFact: Central Park spans 843 acres and was designed by Frederick Law Olmsted and Calvert Vaux in 1858. It contains over 18,000 trees and receives 42 million visits per year.

tiles.name: Harlem
tiles.funFact: The Harlem Renaissance of the 1920s produced legendary artists like Langston Hughes, Duke Ellington, and Zora Neale Hurston, transforming American culture forever.

tiles.name: Upper West Side
tiles.funFact: The Upper West Side is home to Lincoln Center, the world's leading performing arts complex with 11 resident organizations including the Metropolitan Opera.

tiles.name: PATH Train
tiles.funFact: The PATH train connects Manhattan to New Jersey through tunnels beneath the Hudson River, carrying over 80 million passengers per year since opening in 1908.

tiles.name: Upper East Side
tiles.funFact: The Upper East Side's Museum Mile along Fifth Avenue contains nine world-class museums within one mile, including the Metropolitan Museum of Art.

tiles.name: Williamsburg
tiles.funFact: Williamsburg was originally an independent city and became part of Brooklyn in 1855. Its transformation into a creative hub began in the 1990s with artists seeking affordable studio space.

tiles.name: NYC Water
tiles.funFact: New York City's water supply system delivers over 1 billion gallons daily from reservoirs up to 125 miles away, and is so pure it is one of only five major US cities that doesn't need to filter it.

tiles.name: DUMBO
tiles.funFact: DUMBO stands for "Down Under the Manhattan Bridge Overpass." Artists in the 1970s invented the unappealing name hoping to discourage developers from gentrifying the area.

tiles.name: Tribeca
tiles.funFact: Tribeca, short for "Triangle Below Canal Street," is one of the most expensive neighborhoods in Manhattan and hosts the prestigious Tribeca Film Festival founded by Robert De Niro.

tiles.name: Flatiron
tiles.funFact: The Flatiron Building, completed in 1902, was one of NYC's first skyscrapers at 22 stories. Its distinctive triangular shape causes strong wind gusts at street level.

tiles.name: Hudson Yards
tiles.funFact: Hudson Yards is the largest private real estate development in U.S. history at $25 billion. Its centerpiece, the Vessel, contains 154 interconnecting flights of stairs.

tiles.name: Brooklyn Terminal
tiles.funFact: Brooklyn's Atlantic Terminal sits where the Long Island Rail Road began service in 1836, making it one of the oldest railroad terminals in the United States.

tiles.name: Empire State
tiles.funFact: The Empire State Building was constructed in just 410 days during the Great Depression, employing 3,400 workers daily. It has its own zip code: 10118.

tiles.name: Statue of Liberty
tiles.funFact: The Statue of Liberty was a gift from France in 1886, standing 305 feet tall. Her torch was originally designed as a lighthouse visible 24 miles out to sea.
